data.title: Gold prices could hit record highs of over US$4,900 next year: forecasts
data.description:
Several major banks forecast that gold prices will shake off a recent correction and hit an all-time high next year, with ongoing strong demand from central banks and anticipated US Federal Reserve rate cuts likely to buoy the market.
Deutsche Bank has raised its 2026 average gold price forecast to US$4,450 per ounce from US$4,000 previously, setting a projected yearly trading range of US$3,950 to US$4,950, according to a research note released on Wednesday.
“Stabilising investor flow and technical measures indicate a positioning correction has completed,” said Michael Hsueh, a research analyst at the bank.
“Overall growth in demand outpaces supply”, with third-quarter data indicating “a continued central bank bid”, he added.
The projection comes as gold prices make a choppy rebound after a sharp plunge last month from an all-time high of more than US$4,380 per ounce.
The market for the precious metal had seen a historic rally earlier this year, with a buying spree by global central banks pushing up prices alongside strong demand from investors seeking safe-haven assets amid geopolitical tensions and concerns over the long-term stability of the US dollar.
At midday on Friday, the spot price of gold stood at US$4,188 per ounce.
Daan Struyven, co-head of global commodities research at Goldman Sachs, said the investment bank expected gold prices to rise nearly 20 per cent to reach US$4,900 per ounce by the end of next year.
“The two drivers of the [2025] rally, we think, will be repeated in [2026],” he said in an interview with Bloomberg on Wednesday.


Struyven cited structurally higher central bank purchases since the freezing of Russia’s assets in 2022, as well as expectations of continued Fed rate cuts next year, which would lower the opportunity cost of holding non-yielding assets such as gold.
“We get support both from central bank buying and from private investors,” he added.
The US Federal Reserve resumed its rate-cutting cycle in September, having already delivered two quarter-percentage-point cuts this year. According to the CME Group’s FedWatch tool, markets saw a more than 80 per cent probability of another rate cut at next month’s policy meeting as of Friday.
China’s central bank extended its gold-buying streak for a 12th straight month in October, according to the latest official data, adding 30,000 ounces to its reserves to bring its total stock to 74.09 million ounces, valued at US$297.2 billion.
Bank of America also forecast that gold prices would post further gains next year, potentially reaching up to US$5,000 per ounce, in a note published in late October.
Though the market had become overbought, triggering the recent correction, history suggested that prices were likely to recover, according to Michael Widmer, the bank’s head of metals research.
“The magnitude of the current rally is not out of the ordinary when compared to any of the gold bull markets since 1970,” Widmer wrote in the note. Price drops of about 10 per cent were not unusual, he added, with the metal typically “posting strong rallies thereafter”.

data.title: China EV makers’ profit woes fuel market anxiety over challenging 2026
data.description:
Investors in Chinese electric vehicle (EV) stocks, who had been hoping for a strong earnings season to provide fresh tailwinds, instead face disappointing results that have stoked anxiety about what lies ahead.
The sector was riding high – with Xpeng’s year-to-date gain exceeding 130 per cent earlier this month – buoyed by a surge in global risk appetite and an improved outlook for Chinese assets. But signs of pressure at even established firms like BYD have raised new questions over the industry’s profitability after its rapid expansion.
Attention is now shifting to how Chinese EV makers will fare next year, with domestic demand expected to soften as government policy support wanes. Earnings may suffer further with costs seen rising and discounts for consumers likely to continue.
“We expect the demand environment in 1Q 2026 to be challenging, particularly after nearly two years of national trade-in and scrappage policies” that boosted EV purchases, said Bing Yuan, a fund manager at Edmond de Rothschild Asset Management. Competition could intensify, hurting margins into next year, she added.
Traders quickly turned against the best‑performing stocks as results fell short. Xpeng shares dropped 10 per cent in Hong Kong the day after it reported continued losses and issued weak guidance. Zhejiang Leapmotor Technology touched its lowest level since April after its profit came in at less than 65 per cent of the analyst estimate even as sales nearly doubled.
Li Auto and Nio were among others issuing fourth-quarter revenue and vehicle delivery forecasts that missed market expectations. The outlooks suggest sluggish consumer demand in what is a critical period for carmakers striving to hit annual sales targets.
Analysts had projected a bump in deliveries towards the end of this year, given that taxes on EV purchases will be phasing back in from 2026 following years of exemptions. Things are only likely to worsen next year, with Bloomberg Intelligence estimating China’s EV growth will slow to 13 per cent from 27 per cent this year.
Geely Automobile Holdings this month launched a rebate of up to 15,000 yuan (US$2,119) to make up for the scaling back of tax breaks. Other makers have done so as well, including Li Auto and Xiaomi.

Such offers, combined with rising battery costs, would be “headwinds for margins”, said Daisy Li, a fund manager at EFG Asset Management. So earnings pressure would remain even as companies started to move away from fierce price wars on a push from Beijing’s “anti‑involution” campaign, she added.
Manufacturers of lower-priced vehicles like BYD, Geely and Leapmotor were likely better positioned for next year’s market downturn, said Xiao Feng, co-head of China industrial research at CLSA Hong Kong.
“We continue to see a clear downgrading trend, with buyers who once chose mid‑ to high‑end models now shifting towards mass‑market cars,” Feng said. Such models also sold well outside China, he added.
Some makers are in fact expanding in overseas markets where they can sell cars at higher prices as a way to bolster margins. BYD’s overseas sales volume more than doubled from a year earlier in the third quarter, fuelled by demand in Europe and Latin America. Geely expects sales volume outside China to surge as much as 80 per cent next year.
Others are looking beyond EVs for future growth. Xpeng said it planned to mass-produce humanoid robots by the end of 2026, while Li Auto said it would aim to transform vehicles into “embodied AI” robots.
Such efforts may take time to bear fruit. For now, China’s EV segment faces a murky outlook.
“Investors would be reluctant to increase investments in this sector without policy clarity for 2026,” UBS Securities Asia analysts including Paul Gong said in a note. “We stay cautious on the sector, especially the outperformers under the stimulus cycle.”

data.title: Singapore names Gavin Lee permanent head football coach after Hong Kong win
data.description:
Gavin Lee was on Friday named as permanent head coach of the Singapore men’s national football team.
The 35-year-old was officially unveiled at a press conference at Jalan Besar Stadium.
He is the Lions’ first Singaporean permanent head coach since V Sundramoorthy was appointed in 2016.
Earlier on Thursday, reports said that Lee had been offered the full-time role and was expected to put pen to paper.
This move comes hot on the heels of Lee leading the Singapore men’s national football team to a historic victory, which saw them qualify for the 2027 Asian Cup in Saudi Arabia on merit for the first time.
Singapore’s only appearance came in 1984 when they took part automatically as hosts.
In a winner-takes-all clash earlier this month, the Lions, the colloquial name for the men’s football team, came from behind to beat Hong Kong 2-1 to seal their berth in the tournament.

At 28, Lee became the youngest head coach in Singapore Premier League history when he took the helm of BG Tampines Rovers.
He joined the national team in March 2024 as part of then-Lions head coach Tsutomu Ogura’s back room staff. Lee eventually became an assistant to the Japanese tactician.
When Ogura departed in June due to personal reasons, Lee was appointed interim head coach.
Before Lee’s appointment, a number of candidates were linked to the post, including former Tottenham Hotspur assistant coach Nick Montgomery and Jesus Casas, the former head coach of the Iraq national team.
This story was first published by CNA

data.title: How to use juniper berries in marinades for strong meats and to add flavour to sauerkraut
data.description:
Juniper berries taste like how pine trees smell – sharp, resinous and clean.
If you have ever had a gin and tonic, or a traditional martini – as opposed to one made with vodka or sake – you will recognise the taste of juniper, which is the main flavour component of gin.
The so-called berry is actually the cone part of the juniper shrub, which is part of the cypress family. In areas where the plant grows – primarily in mountainous regions throughout the world, including Europe and India – the juniper berry is often used fresh.
For longer storage, though, the berries are dried and cured. Before using them in a dish, crack or bruise the berries to release their flavour.

Juniper oil is extracted from the berries and is used in holistic health treatments as a diuretic, to relieve stress, and to help with joint and skin problems.
In the kitchen, juniper is often paired with strong-tasting meats to counteract their gaminess and heaviness.
Use them for a marinade…
For a marinade for venison, mutton or wild game, combine crushed juniper berries with red wine, bay leaves, peppercorns, garlic and shallots, then mix with the meat, which should be lightly salted.
After you cook the meat, strain the marinade, then simmer the liquid with something sweet (to balance the acidity), such as red currant jelly, sugar, honey or maple syrup. Season to taste with salt, then drizzle the sauce over the meat before serving.

…and to flavour sauerkraut/choucroute
I also like to flavour home-made sauerkraut with crushed juniper berries, and improve the flavour of canned sauerkraut, which can be pretty insipid.
Drain the cabbage and rinse it briefly if it is too salty. Use your hands to squeeze out as much moisture as possible. Remove and discard the skin from a chunk of pancetta and cut it into cubes (you could use streaky bacon instead).
Fry the pancetta until slightly brown, then add unsalted butter to the pan; if using bacon, drain off most of the fat before adding the butter. Stir in some sliced onion and garlic cloves, and cook until tender.
Add the drained sauerkraut, dry white wine, a stalk of fresh thyme, a bay leaf (broken into two pieces), caraway seeds, lightly crushed juniper berries and whole black peppercorns. Simmer for about 30 minutes.
This is delicious with grilled meat, such as sausages, but it can also be turned into an easy-to-make variation of choucroute, a classic dish from the Alsace region of France.

The traditional version uses smoked ham or ham hocks, duck, and at least two types of fresh sausages. For a much easier version that has almost as much flavour, brown fresh sausages, bury them in the simmering sauerkraut and continue to cook for about 30 minutes.
While the choucroute is cooking, boil new potatoes until tender. Serve the choucroute and potatoes with mustard, horseradish, and lots of beer or white wine.
This content first appeared in Post Magazine in 2004 and 2013.

data.title: Posters urging South Koreans to join Ukraine war slammed as ‘Russian hoax’
data.description:
An advertisement offering lucrative pay and citizenship to South Koreans willing to join Ukraine’s army has been rejected by Kyiv as a “Russian hoax”, even as Seoul confirmed for the first time the death of a citizen who fought against Moscow in the long-running war.
The conflict-stricken nation’s response came after photographs of posters plastered on a lamp post in Seongnam, South Korea’s Gyeonggi province, circulated online, detailing supposed enlistment benefits including high wages and choice of battalion.
The flyers also touted another perk: “the legal right to kill North Koreans”.
The materials featured contact information for the Ukrainian embassy in Seoul, which said some South Koreans had reached out to the mission after seeing the posters.
Ukraine’s foreign ministry quickly distanced itself from the images, denouncing them as a part of Moscow’s “disinformation campaign”.

“We officially refute this next Russian hoax – neither the Ukrainian foreign ministry nor the Ukrainian embassy in the Republic of Korea distributed such leaflets,” ministry spokesman Heorhii Tykhyi said, as quoted by The Korea Herald.
Kyiv’s diplomatic mission in Seoul told the publication that it kept receiving “calls from regular Koreans” asking about the job offer, and “we just inform them this is not the embassy’s area of work”.
The clarification on the purported enlistment drive came as a South Korean foreign ministry official confirmed on Thursday that a volunteer fighter from the country was killed in the war, which began with Russia’s full-scale invasion of Ukraine in 2022.
A consular official from Seoul’s embassy in Kyiv attended a funeral in the Ukrainian capital on Tuesday for the South Korean man apparently killed during combat in the Donetsk region in May, according to the Yonhap news agency, adding that consular help was provided to the victim’s family.
Previously, Seoul had never confirmed reports of its nationals fighting for Ukraine, some of whom died in the war.
In September, Foreign Minister Cho Hyun said South Korea would “seriously consider” ways to help Ukraine in its post-war rehabilitation.

The country’s nuclear-armed neighbour, North Korea, has supplied weapons and troops to Russia, which is studying a peace proposal put forward by the United States as President Donald Trump continues his push to end the nearly four-year-old war.
In August, North Korean leader Kim Jong-un honoured more than 100 soldiers from his country who were killed in the conflict, calling them “great heroes and patriots”.
President Vladimir Putin held a phone call with Kim that same month and commended North Korea’s help in “liberating” the Kursk region in western Russia in the war against Ukraine.
Moscow and Pyongyang have rapidly advanced their military ties in recent years.

data.title: Hong Kong will emerge stronger from Tai Po fire tragedy
data.description:
Feel strongly about these letters, or any other aspects of the news? Share your views by emailing us your Letter to the Editor at letters@scmp.com or filling in this Google form. Submissions should not exceed 400 words
It was only until I saw traces of smoke in the sky even from a swimming pool in Sha Tin that I was struck by the scale of the Tai Po fire. As the death toll continues to rise, I would like to offer my sincerest condolences to the families of the victims.
In recent days, stories, videos and images of the bereaved wailing in agony and shattered families have flooded social media. The reactions to the blaze raise an interesting question: while it is essential that we stay up to date, is being fully immersed in the coverage of the tragedy beneficial? Some of these somewhat sensationalist stories risk jeopardising the mental health of Hong Kong people.
The ability to come up with both short-term and long-term solutions thus stands out as invaluable in crises like this. Consoling the bereaved and those living close by is of utmost importance. It is equally heartening to see billionaires and businesses donating millions of dollars to help with rescue and rehabilitation efforts in the wake of the deadly blaze. Some are offering free stays at hotel rooms, while university students and community organisations are volunteering their help and donating supplies such as towels, bedsheets and toothbrushes to address the immediate needs of the now homeless residents.
And as the saying goes, we emerge sadder and wiser from every tragedy. Hong Kong is in dire need of improving fire safety with multiple blazes and the Tai Po tragedy. While the cause of the blaze is still under investigation, abandoning bamboo scaffolding in favour of steel ones, increasing surprise inspections of construction sites to combat violations of safety regulations such as smoking or using polystyrene boards to cover windows are all promising solutions worth considering and implementing as soon as possible.
As for long-term education on the importance of fire safety, mandatory fire drills in strict adherence to guidelines from the Fire Services Department at all buildings could be an effective solution.
fellow Hongkonger, I express my deepest sympathy with the bereaved. The road to recovery will be full of trials and tribulations. Yet, let us not forget the Lion Rock spirit — Hong Kong will emerge stronger from this tragedy.
Lucas Lee, Sha Tin
City must follow international best practice on prevention
As a former member of the Hong Kong and Kowloon Wharf and Godown Wharf Police, the catastrophic fire at Wang Fuk Court in Hong Kong on November 26 has shaken me to the core. Not only am I horrified by the escalating loss of life in this multiple-apartment building blaze, but I am profoundly saddened by the knowledge that such devastation was avoidable.
Reports indicate that exterior bamboo scaffolding, green netting and polystyrene foam panels used during renovations are suspected to have contributed to the rapid spread of flames. Yet what troubles me most is not only the combustible materials but the apparent absence of fundamental life safety systems in case of fire. These structures lacked fire suppression sprinklers, and each building was designed with only a single emergency exit stairwell.
This tragedy may prove to be not only the worst high-rise residential fire in Hong Kong’s history but among the deadliest apartment building fires in the world. NFPA International, widely recognised as a leader in building fire life safety codes, has long recognised the standard that buildings exceeding 75 feet in height (about seven stories) should have at least two emergency exit stairwells. This is not a theoretical guideline – it is a life-saving measure.
I understand that when the complex was originally built, its builders were following the prevailing building codes. However, had such a basic requirement been integrated into the building design, hundreds of residents might have had a fighting chance to survive.
High-rise fires are not new to history. From the Grenfell Tower fire in London to blazes in São Paulo and Shanghai, the lessons have been written in ash and sorrow. Yet time and again, those lessons are ignored until tragedy strikes anew. The Wang Fuk Court fire is a grim reminder that complacency in design and enforcement is paid for in human lives.
The responsibility lies not only with builders, contractors and developers but with regulators and policymakers who must enforce international best practices. Fire does not respect borders, and neither should safety standards. Hong Kong, like every global city, must recognise that the cost of prevention is far less than the cost of catastrophe.
As someone who has dedicated my career to high-rise security and fire life safety, I urge immediate action. The world cannot afford to treat this disaster as an isolated incident. It must become a turning point – a moment when governments, architects and safety professionals recommit to the principles that protect life above all else.
The residents of Wang Fuk Court deserved better. Their memory demands that we do better.
Geoff Craighead, CPP, Portland, Oregon, US
Deadly blaze has united a community in grief and action
The tragic fire in Tai Po has left our community in a state of profound grief. My deepest condolences, and those of so many across Hong Kong, are with the victims and the families who have lost their loved ones. As a solicitor, I wish to offer my support and to provide any legal or other assistance I can to those affected by this devastating incident.
In the face of such sorrow, one thing has been truly inspiring: how this tragedy has brought people together. We have witnessed an outstanding outpouring of compassion, with individuals from all walks of life coming forth to offer help in whatever capacity they could. It is particularly heartening to see the visible and active role played by our ethnic minority communities in this collective aid effort. This solidarity showcases the very best of our city’s spirit, reminding us that our shared humanity transcends all backgrounds.
To ensure such a catastrophe is avoided in the future, we must learn from this event and seek out every possible avenue for improvement.
Mainland China has made remarkable advancements in firefighting technology, particularly in the development of sophisticated firefighting drones capable of tackling blazes in high-rise structures. Could such advanced drone technology be shared with our Fire Services Department? Integrating such innovative tools could enhance our emergency response capabilities, potentially saving countless lives in the future.
Ahmed Ashfaq, Tsim Sha Tsui

data.title: Trump says US permanently pausing migration from ‘Third World Countries’
data.description:
US President Donald Trump said on Thursday that his administration will work to “permanently pause” migration from all “Third World Countries” to allow the US system to fully recover.
Trump did not identify any countries by name or explain what he meant by third-world countries or “permanently pause”. He said the plan would include cases approved under former president Joe Biden’s administration.
“I will permanently pause migration from all Third World Countries to allow the U.S. system to fully recover, terminate all of the millions of Biden illegal admissions, including those signed by Sleepy Joe Biden’s Autopen, and remove anyone who is not a net asset to the United States,” he said on his social media platform, Truth Social.
Trump said he would end all federal benefits and subsidies for “noncitizens”, adding he would “denaturalize migrants who undermine domestic tranquility” and deport any foreign national deemed a public charge, security risk, or “non-compatible with Western civilization”.
White House and US Citizenship and Immigration Services did not immediately respond to Reuters’ requests for comment.
Trump’s comments followed the death of a National Guard member who had been shot near the White House in an ambush investigators say was carried out by an Afghan national.
Earlier, officials from the Department of Homeland Security officials said that Trump had ordered a widespread review of asylum cases approved under Biden’s administration and Green Cards issued to citizens of 19 countries.
The alleged gunman was granted asylum this year under Trump, according to a US government file seen by Reuters.
The US Citizenship and Immigration Services on Wednesday stopped processing all immigration requests relating to Afghan nationals indefinitely.
“These goals will be pursued with the aim of achieving a major reduction in illegal and disruptive populations,” Trump said.

data.title: Malaysian police chief urges more caning in schools, rues loss of ‘fear factor’
data.description:
A Malaysian police chief has called for the wider imposition of caning in schools, arguing that the “fear factor” among students has eroded at a time when violent incidents, including bullying-related deaths, are drawing alarm in the country.
Selangor state police chief Shazeli Kahar said caning should be considered in a phased and “controlled” manner to prevent teacher abuse, and was needed to address what he described as a worrying rise in student-related crime across the state.
His remarks follow several high-profile cases inside educational institutions this year, including the killing of a student in Bandar Utama in October, which prompted a heated national debate on school safety. The incident also followed other reports of serious bullying, group assaults and intimidation involving students in different states.
Speaking at an anti-bullying event in Selangor, Shazeli said police had arrested over 120 primary and secondary school pupils between January and October.
“There are various categories of offences … extortion, causing injury, fights and students using words they shouldn’t. Some cases even involve elements of gangsterism,” he told reporters on Thursday. “What worries us most is when fights escalate into riots… If there are gang elements, it becomes even more concerning.”

Shazeli said disciplinary measures in schools had weakened over the years, reducing teachers’ deterrent powers.
He said the “fear factor” had also waned due to a slackening in enforcing rules. “These should exist so teachers can manage discipline,” he said, calling for careful use of corporal punishment.
“Caning has various categories… minimal strokes, the number of strokes. It should be reintroduced in stages and controlled clearly to avoid misuse of power among teachers.”
Malaysia still allows limited caning in schools under education ministry rules introduced more than two decades ago, but the practice has been increasingly discouraged, with many schools moving away from physical punishment following child rights concerns and several high-profile abuse cases.
In February, the education ministry clarified that only principals and formally designated teachers could cane students under a strict standard operating procedure. Malaysian Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim told parliament in October that he personally supported caning as a method of disciplining students.

A 2019 public survey found that while most parents recalled being caned during their own schooling, fewer than half supported its continued use.
Still, the push has stirred debate online, with some questioning whether harsher discipline would address deeper systemic issues.
“What needs to be fixed is not the cane but an education system that fails to build moral grounding,” a user said in a social media post. Another user said: “Even with a thick cane, if there is no discipline in the system, the problems remain.”

data.title: Self-made China milk tea entrepreneur marries wealthy solar heiress, captures public attention
data.description:
The upcoming wedding of the founder of a renowned milk tea company from a grass-roots background and the daughter of a wealthy family that owns a leading solar energy firm has captivated the Chinese public and is trending online.
Zhang Junjie, the driving force behind the Chinese milk tea brand Chagee, and his bride Gao Haichun, whose parents are the founders of Trina Solar, will celebrate their nuptials at the Zhongwu Hotel in Changzhou, eastern Jiangsu province, on December 15, according to Jiemian News.
Both Zhang and Gao are 32 years old.
Gao, a Brown University graduate, has been hailed as the most beautiful second-generation figure in China’s solar energy sector, often referred to as the “Solar Goddess.” She also holds an executive position at her parents’ company and is their only child.

Trina Solar is publicly listed in both New York and Shanghai.
What has particularly fascinated internet users is Zhang’s inspiring journey from humble beginnings.
Born into poverty in Kunming, Yunnan, southwest China, Zhang became an orphan at the age of 10. Information regarding his parents’ deaths and details about any relatives remain undisclosed.
With minimal formal education, Zhang began working as an apprentice at a milk tea store in Kunming at 17.
He learned to read and write at 18. At that store, he advanced from manager’s assistant to manager, and then regional supervisor. Within five years, he became an agent of that milk tea brand and a franchise partner.

Zhang also had a brief tenure from 2015 to 2017 at a Shanghai-based robotics company, where he handled external cooperation.
In November 2017, Zhang launched Chagee, a milk tea brand inspired by traditional Chinese aesthetics.
While many of his peers focused on fruit milk tea, Zhang distinguished himself by prioritising fresh tea leaves as his primary ingredient.
He expressed to the media his ambition to rival Starbucks with his Chinese tea offerings.
With 6,000 stores both in China and abroad, Chagee was officially listed on NASDAQ in April.

Many of Zhang’s employees admire his unwavering dedication to his career.
“To this day, he hasn’t bought a car or a house. He lacks materialistic desires and aspires to leave a mark in history,” an anonymous employee acquainted with Zhang shared with Jiemian News.
Zhang and Gao first crossed paths at a networking event for young entrepreneurs, and they registered their marriage in June.
On November 21, Zhang addressed a rumour circulating online—that he had previously married a tycoon’s disabled daughter and had a child with her—by releasing a statement on social media.
This unfounded rumour emerged late last year, leading a Shanghai court to mandate a public apology to Zhang from the individual responsible earlier this year.
Despite this clarification, many internet users continued to discuss the rumour following reports of Zhang’s wedding in mid-November.

“I feel it’s essential to speak out openly. I want to clarify that I have never been married before meeting Haichun. I aim to protect my wife and family from any defamation stemming from this rumour,” Zhang stated on social media.
The couple’s impending marriage has sparked considerable public interest on mainland social media, going viral with 87 million views on a leading platform.
“He started from scratch. Now, he’s marrying a beautiful and wealthy woman. He’s someone every man envies,” commented one online observer.
“He has quickly risen in the business realm. He is undoubtedly a genius!” praised another.
A third user remarked: “It seems as if the inspiring hero from a novel has come to life.”

data.title: Deadly boat accident cover-up reveals corruption in China’s fishing industry
data.description:
A local government official in northeast China has been accused of covering up a fishing boat accident and preventing the vessel’s owner from calling the police for help, and investigations sparked by the incident have exposed a series of corruption cases related to fishing industry safety.
The accident took place on November 10. A boat registered in Yingkou, Liaoning province, capsized while crew members were fishing, causing 11 to fall into the water. Two were rescued and nine have been reported missing, according to the Liaoning Daily on Wednesday.
Official investigations found corruption in the approval process and arrangements for the fishing trip.
The boat owner, surnamed Zheng, used other people’s qualifications to get past safety checks. During the trip, he arranged for two people who lacked valid crew certificates to board the boat. This meant the fishing vessel failed to meet requirements for offshore operations, resulting in serious safety risks, the report said. Zheng was detained by local police on November 16.
The party secretary of a local district fisheries and marine bureau not only delayed rescue but also had a staff member convince the boat owner not to call the police in an attempt to cover up the truth. The report said the official and staff member, surnamed Xu and Chi, respectively, were awaiting trial.
On November 11, the Chinese embassy in South Korea said in a statement that two fishing vessels had capsized in waters near the country in the two days prior, leaving two dead and 12 missing. One of the incidents the embassy described matched the details in the Yingkou report.
On November 10, the Liaoning provincial government held a video conference highlighting the importance of fishing safety, demanding local governments adopt a “highly responsible attitude and take decisive, forceful measures to curb the frequent occurrence of accidents”, according to the report.


Local governments were also asked to investigate the causes of these incidents.
“Are there instances of partially deploying central government policies? Is there a tendency for local officials to abandon their responsibilities? Is there a corruption problem? Is there a pattern for empty slogans and poor execution?” the provincial authorities asked, according to the report.
The campaign is focused on key links in the fishing industry, including vessel inspection, fishing port supervision, crew certification and port transfer approval. It targets law enforcement personnel, vessel owners and captains and fishing port operators.
As of Monday, disciplinary authorities at all levels in the province had filed cases against 162 people, and police had detained 104 and solved 45 cases. Another 52 people had turned themselves in to disciplinary authorities, the report said.
Besides corruption, authorities found other illegal practices in the cases. They include buying boat certificates from owners and selling them to people without the legal document, who in turn bribed officials in charge of approving fishing trips to change the database and give the boats legal status.
One official who turned himself in told authorities he had accepted four boxes of seafood and two cartons of cigarettes to ignore illegal fishing operations.
“I know they wanted to maintain a good relationship with me so that I would turn a blind eye to their lack of safety equipment or qualified crew on their boats,” he said.
Investigations have found that those who took bribes were more lenient towards boats that were overstaffed, had inadequate firefighting equipment or sneaked out during the night, according to the report.
Following repeated calls from President Xi Jinping to strengthen safety practices, the 14th Five-Year National Work Safety Plan, published in 2022, vowed to “strengthen safety oversight of high-risk fishing vessels and fishing port waters and rigorously crack down on practices such as overloading, operating beyond safe wind limits or designated zones, and illegal activities involving unregistered, unlicensed or unregulated fishing vessels”.
To address regulatory loopholes, local governments in Liaoning have established supervision stations and upgraded an intelligent supervision system for fishing vessels with real-time monitoring of vessel departure and arrival reports, sailing routes and other critical data. Public hotlines are also open for the public to report violations and other concerns 24 hours a day, according to the report.
